skill.name: lipsync
skill.description: Routes lip-sync requests to RunComfy CLI models for existing videos, portrait avatars, and script-driven generated videos.
skill.summary: Create lip-synced videos with RunComfy models from video, portrait, audio, or script inputs.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most were Markdown backticks, model names, headings, or documentation links rather than executable Ruby, weak crypto, or reconnaissance. The real risk is moderate because the skill authorizes RunComfy CLI execution, sends user media references to external RunComfy services, and documents local API token storage.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Invocation to RunComfy
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for runcomfy commands and provides multiple runcomfy run examples. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it executes an external tool and should remain limited to the documented command scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command examples clearly show external command use. The command scope is limited to runcomfy, so this is a controlled operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: User Media Sent to External Model Service
medium_findings.description: The documented workflow sends video, image, and audio URLs to RunComfy model endpoints, then downloads generated outputs. This is expected behavior but creates privacy and third-party processing risk for sensitive media.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples and How it works section directly describe external API calls and output downloads. The endpoints are documented RunComfy services, with no evidence of covert exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Local API Token Storage Documented
low_findings.description: The skill notes that runcomfy login stores an API token under the user configuration directory. The file mode mitigation is documented, but local credential storage remains a minor exposure if the host is compromised.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and file mode are explicitly documented on one line. This is a real but mitigated credential-handling concern, not evidence of credential theft.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick and Weak-Crypto Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick, weak cryptographic algorithm, and reconnaissance findings map to Markdown formatting, lip-sync model names, exit-code documentation, and route guidance. No evidence found of Ruby execution, cryptographic code, or system reconnaissance logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms these lines are prose, Markdown links, command examples, or model descriptions. No executable Ruby, crypto implementation, or host probing behavior appears in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Scope Allows RunComfy Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows Bash execution for runcomfy commands only. This should be monitored because CLI tools can perform network calls and file writes within their own behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool declaration and security section both define the command scope. The scope is narrow, but it still enables command execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Processing Through RunComfy Endpoints
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI workflow posts to RunComfy model APIs, polls status, and downloads output URLs. Users should avoid submitting private media without proper approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is described directly in the skill documentation. It is intentional service use, not hidden network activity.

content.value_statement: Lip-sync workflows require choosing the right model for each media input. This skill routes video, portrait, audio, and script requests to the matching RunComfy endpoint.
actual_capabilities: Selects a RunComfy lip-sync route based on video, portrait, audio, or script inputs.
Provides RunComfy CLI commands for Sync Labs, OmniHuman, Kling, and Creatify workflows.
Supports existing video plus audio workflows for mouth synchronization.
Supports portrait plus audio workflows for talking-head avatar generation.
Documents script-only options that generate speech and synced video together.
Includes safety guidance for consent, voice provenance, and allowed endpoints.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and a valid RunComfy account or token.
Does not verify consent, rights, or identity ownership automatically.
Quality depends on input media clarity, audio length, and selected model tier.
Processes media through external RunComfy services, so private content needs review.

use_cases.title: Dub a Product Video
use_cases.description: Use an existing product video and translated voiceover to create a mouth-synced localized version.

use_cases.title: Create an Avatar Presenter
use_cases.description: Turn an approved portrait and narration track into a presenter-style video for lessons or announcements.

use_cases.title: Generate Scripted Social Clips
use_cases.description: Start from a short script and use a text-to-video lip-sync route when no recorded audio exists.

prompt_templates.title: Sync an Existing Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this source video and this voiceover audio to create a lip-synced version. Choose the best default RunComfy route.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Talking Portrait
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a talking-head video from this approved portrait and narration audio. Use the route that best fits still images.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Quality and Cost
prompt_templates.prompt: I need lip sync for ten short videos. Recommend a RunComfy model route that balances quality, cost, and batch production.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Language Launch
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workflow for one approved presenter identity and five language voiceovers. Include route choice, input checks, and consent safeguards.

output_examples.input: A brand video and a Spanish narration track need synchronized mouth movement.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends Sync Labs sync v2 Pro for the best existing-video mouth sync and prepares a RunComfy CLI workflow.

output_examples.input: A creator has an approved headshot and a product pitch voiceover.
output_examples.output: The skill routes the task to OmniHuman because the input is a still portrait plus audio.

output_examples.input: A short ad script is available, but there is no recorded audio file.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests a text-to-video lip-sync route that generates speech and synchronized video in one pass.

best_practices: Confirm consent for both the face and the voice before running any lip-sync job.
Use clean speech audio and match its duration closely to the source video.
Keep private media on approved storage and review RunComfy processing requirements first.
anti_patterns: Do not target real people, public figures, or private individuals without clear permission.
Do not submit confidential media to external services without approval.
Do not use script-only routes when exact synchronization to a recorded voice is required.

faq.question: What inputs does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports source video plus audio, portrait image plus audio, and script-only generation routes.

faq.question: Does it install RunComfy automatically?
faq.answer: No. The user must install and authenticate the RunComfy CLI before running jobs.

faq.question: Can it handle professional dubbing?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill recommends Sync Labs sync v2 Pro for high-quality existing-video dubbing.

faq.question: Can it create avatar videos from a photo?
faq.answer: Yes. It routes approved portrait and audio inputs to OmniHuman-style avatar workflows.

faq.question: Does it enforce consent rules?
faq.answer: No. It documents consent requirements, but operators must verify rights before use.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill runs an external CLI, uses network model APIs, and documents local token storage.
